claim: The 70.1% hapax ratio is substantially artifactual: a systematic suffix composed of word-final glyphs drawn from {y, n, l, r, o} acts as a grammatical or cipher suffix, and stripping these final glyphs from all word tokens reduces the effective vocabulary size and hapax ratio to levels consistent with natural language (~30â€“45% hapax).
evidence_for: Word-final glyph set {y, n, l, r, o} is highly constrained and positionally predictable â€” consistent with suffixal affixes rather than freely distributed glyphs
Belief score 0.37 for suffix-stripping reducing hapax ratio, but higher scores (0.79â€“0.87) confirm positional constraints are cipher-structural
Stripping inflectional suffixes in Latin reduces hapax rates significantly in comparable medieval corpora
evidence_against: Prior attempt scored only 0.37 confidence, suggesting the reduction may not bring hapax to natural-language levels
If suffixes encode meaningful cipher content rather than grammar, stripping them would destroy information rather than reveal stems
Average word length of 5.03 is short; removing 1â€“2 final glyphs may fragment many tokens
test: For each unique word type, strip all trailing glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} (up to 2 glyphs); recompute vocabulary size, hapax count, and hapax ratio on the stemmed corpus; also recompute Zipf exponent and entropy on stemmed tokens.
test_metric: Hapax ratio of stemmed corpus; Zipf exponent of stemmed corpus; reduction in unique vocabulary size
test_threshold: Stemmed hapax ratio < 45% and Zipf exponent moving closer to 1.0 would support systematic suffix hypothesis; ratio remaining > 60% would suggest hapaxes are not suffix-driven.

tests_run.test: for each unique word type, strip all trailing glyphs belonging to {y, n, l, r, o} (up to 2 glyphs);
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: If suffix-stripping halves the hapax rate and improves the Zipf fit, it strongly supports a morphologically inflected plaintext (Latin/Italian) with a predictable cipher suffix, narrowing both the language and cipher type simultaneously.
